county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as one of the original Dakota Territory counties. Named for Charles Mix, Commissioner of Indian Affairs. Contains part of Yankton Sioux Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Charles Mix County Register of Deeds in Lake Andes holds some of SD's earliest land records from 1862. As an original Dakota Territory county, territorial-era records are also at SDSHS. Yankton Sioux Reservation occupies the eastern portion — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls, allotment records, and agency records for the Yankton Sioux. Fort Randall (1856-1892) was in northern Charles Mix County; military records at NARA document soldiers and their families. Czech (Bohemian) settlers were significant — check Catholic parish records and Czech fraternal organizations (ZCBJ lodge records). BLM GLO homestead records document original entries. Charles Mix County Clerk of Courts has probate and naturalization records. The Lake Andes Wave newspaper at SDSHS is a key source. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1862 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1862. Register of Deeds issued licenses; state registration began 1905.

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Mix County Birth and Death Records
source_profiles.repository: South Dakota Vital Records, Pierre / Register of Deeds
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1905; county-filed records may predate statewide compliance. Certified copies from SD Department of Health Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the Register of Deeds.

source_profiles.source_name: Charles Mix County Clerk of Courts Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, civil and criminal case files from 1862. Clerk of Courts maintains court records for the circuit court.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Charles Mix County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for Charles Mix County including homestead, preemption, timber culture, and cash entries. South Dakota was entirely public domain — GLO records are the primary source for first land ownership.
Federal population schedules for Charles Mix County. Dakota Territory enumerated from 1860. Agricultural schedules (1850-1880) provide additional family and economic data.

source_profiles.repository: South Dakota State Historical Society / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers from Charles Mix County communities. SDSHS holds the largest collection of SD newspapers on microfilm. Chronicling America has digitized SD papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873. Named for Newton Clark, Dakota Territory legislator. Agricultural county with strong Scandinavian settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clark County Register of Deeds in Clark holds deed records from 1873. BLM GLO homestead records at glorecords.blm.gov document original land entries. Norwegian settlers predominated; Norwegian Lutheran church records (Bethany, Spring Lake, etc.) are on FamilySearch and at the Norwegian-American Historical Association (NAHA) in Northfield, MN. Clark County Clerk of Courts has probate, civil, and naturalization records. The Clark County Courier newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county history book (1985) documents pioneer families. Strong agricultural orientation with emphasis on wheat and livestock. Check SDSHS for territorial-era land patent records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county. Home to University of South Dakota (USD, est. 1862). Vermillion is one of the oldest Euro-American settlements in SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Clay County Register of Deeds in Vermillion holds some of SD's oldest land records from 1862. University of South Dakota (USD) Archives and Special Collections in Vermillion is a major research repository with territorial government papers, local manuscript collections, and the I.D. Weeks Library genealogy collection. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Clay County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Vermillion was among the earliest permanent settlements in Dakota Territory. The Vermillion Plain Talk (1875+) is one of SD's oldest newspapers, on microfilm at SDSHS. Norwegian and Scandinavian settlers were prominent; check Norwegian Lutheran church records. The W.H. Over Museum at USD has local history collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Missouri River bottomland attracted early farmers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1877. Watertown is the fifth-largest city in SD. Named for Rev. G.S.S. Codington, territorial legislator. Important railroad and commercial center in northeast SD.
county_metadata.research_tips: Codington County Register of Deeds in Watertown holds deed records from 1877. Watertown was a major Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad hub — railroad colonization records at the Milwaukee Road Historical Association identify immigrant settlers. BLM GLO homestead records cover original land entries. Codington County Clerk of Courts in Watertown has extensive probate and naturalization records. The Codington County Heritage Museum in Watertown has local family files. German and Scandinavian settlers were predominant; Lutheran and Catholic church records are key sources. The Watertown Public Opinion newspaper (1885+) on microfilm at SDSHS is a comprehensive community record. The Kampeska Heritage Museum has pioneer-era collections. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Watertown was a gateway for settlement into northeastern SD.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Boreman County. Contains large portion of Standing Rock Reservation and Cheyenne River Reservation. Named for Dighton Corson, SD Supreme Court justice.
county_metadata.research_tips: Corson County Register of Deeds in McIntosh holds records from 1909. The county encompasses much of Standing Rock Reservation and portions of Cheyenne River Reservation — BIA records at NARA Kansas City are essential, including Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, and agency correspondence. Sitting Bull was killed in 1890 on Standing Rock within what is now Corson County. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims on opened reservation lands. Corson County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. Both tribal and county jurisdictions apply — researchers must consult tribal enrollment records, BIA files, and county records. The McIntosh newspapers at SDSHS cover community history. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. German-Russian and Scandinavian settlers arrived when reservation lands were opened.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1909 to present. Register of Deeds maintains index books and recorded instruments.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1875 during the Black Hills gold rush. Named for Lt. Col. George A. Custer, whose 1874 expedition discovered gold. Home to Custer State Park and Wind Cave National Park.
county_metadata.research_tips: Custer County Register of Deeds in Custer holds deed and mining claim records from 1875. The Black Hills gold rush (1874-1876) brought the first Euro-American settlers in violation of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty. Mining district records document early prospectors and claim holders. BLM GLO homestead and mineral patent records are essential. Custer County Clerk of Courts has probate, civil, and criminal records from the wild gold rush era. The Custer County Chronicle newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. The 1880 Black Hills census is a critical early enumeration. Mining company records identify workers in the gold camps. The Custer County Historical Society has local family files. Wind Cave National Park and Custer State Park records at NARA document later federal land management. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1873 from Hanson County. Mitchell is known for the Corn Palace. Named for Henry C. Davison, Dakota Territory official.
county_metadata.research_tips: Davison County Register of Deeds in Mitchell holds deed records from 1873. Mitchell is a commercial center for south-central SD. The Corn Palace (est. 1892) records at SDSHS document the city's famous attraction and agricultural heritage. BLM GLO homestead records cover original land entries. Davison County Clerk of Courts in Mitchell has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Dakota Wesleyan University archives in Mitchell have local manuscript collections. Czech and German settlers were significant — check Catholic and Lutheran church records. The Mitchell Daily Republic newspaper (1880+) on microfilm at SDSHS is an excellent source. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. Mitchell was a Chicago, Milwaukee, St. Paul and Pacific Railroad town; railroad employment and land records identify settlers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1879. Named for Merritt H. Day, Dakota Territory legislator. Lake region county in northeast SD with numerous glacial lakes.
county_metadata.research_tips: Day County Register of Deeds in Webster holds deed records from 1879. The county includes numerous glacial lakes that attracted settlers. Sisseton-Wahpeton Oyate (Lake Traverse Reservation) borders Day County to the north — BIA records at NARA Kansas City include census rolls and allotment records. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Day County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Norwegian and Swedish settlers predominated; Scandinavian Lutheran church records are essential sources. The Webster Reporter and Farmer newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. The county history book documents pioneer families. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The lake region attracted sportsmen and resort settlers in addition to farmers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1862 as original Dakota Territory county. Named for Jacob S. Deuel, Dakota Territory legislator. Located on the Minnesota border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Deuel County Register of Deeds in Clear Lake holds land records from 1862 — among SD's earliest. As an original Dakota Territory county, early records may also be at SDSHS. Located on the Minnesota border, many families had connections across state lines — check Minnesota county records for related families. BLM GLO homestead records document original claims. Deuel County Clerk of Courts has probate, naturalization, and civil records. Scandinavian (Norwegian, Swedish) settlers predominated; Lutheran church records at ELCA archives (Chicago) and Augustana University (Sioux Falls) are essential. The Clear Lake Courier newspaper is on microfilm at SDSHS. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The county's proximity to Minnesota means researchers should also check Minnesota territorial records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909. Contains large portion of Cheyenne River Indian Reservation. Named for Admiral George Dewey. Organized when reservation lands were opened to settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dewey County Register of Deeds in Timber Lake holds records from 1909. Dewey County encompasses a large portion of Cheyenne River Indian Reservation — BIA records at NARA Kansas City are essential, including Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school records (Cheyenne River Boarding School), and agency correspondence for Minneconjou, Two Kettle, Sans Arc, and Blackfoot Lakota bands. BLM GLO homestead records document settlers who filed claims on opened reservation lands. Dewey County Clerk of Courts has probate and civil records. The Timber Lake Topic newspaper at SDSHS covers community history. Tribal enrollment records at Cheyenne River Agency document Lakota families. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. Ranching families and Lakota allottees are both represented in county records.
